name: El Madroño Miners' Trail
name_original: Aguas rojas del Río Tinto — circular desde El Madroño
description: A difficult 11.6-mile hike from El Madroño, exploring the Rio Tinto's unique red waters and Martian-like mining landscape.
region_intro: Hiking around El Madroño offers exploration of the Sierra de Aracena foothills, characterized by picturesque mountain scenery. The region is notable for the distinctive reddish landscapes shaped by the Río Tinto, a river known for its ancient mining history. Diverse flora, including the namesake madroño trees, contributes to the natural environment. Unique geological formations, such as the rock engravings at Cerro de El Riscal, are also present. Best hiking trails around El Madroño The most popular hiking route is Río Tinto Mining Train Trail, a 5.9 miles (9.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 35 minutes to complete, following the historic railway lines. Another top favourite among local hikers is Río Tinto Greenway Loop, a moderate 5.1 miles (8.3 km) path. This route offers views of the unique "Martian-like" terrain along the Río Tinto. Local hikers also love the On the banks of the Riotinto along the old railway, a 5.6 miles (9.0 km) trail leading through the distinctive Río Tinto landscape, often completed in about 2 hours 36 minutes. Hiking around El Madroño is defined by the Sierra de Aracena foothills, the reddish Río Tinto landscapes, and areas rich in madroño trees. The network of trails offers options for easy paths to more challenging routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in El Madroño are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 70 reviews. More than 330 hikers have used komoot to explore El Madroño's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of hiking trails can I expect around El Madroño?
A: El Madroño offers a diverse range of hiking trails, from easy paths suitable for all abilities to more challenging routes requiring good physical condition. You'll find paths winding through the picturesque Sierra de Aracena foothills and along the distinctive reddish landscapes shaped by the Río Tinto. There are 5 easy routes, 20 moderate routes, and 4 difficult routes available.

Q: Are there any trails that showcase the unique reddish landscapes of the Río Tinto?
A: Yes, several trails highlight the unique 'Martian-like' terrain of the Río Tinto. For a moderate experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1144750763/rio-tinto-greenway-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Tinto Greenway Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers views of this distinctive landscape. Another option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010556547/rio-tinto-mining-train-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Tinto Mining Train Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which follows historic railway lines through the area.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while hiking in El Madroño?
A: The region boasts several unique natural and historical landmarks. You can explore the rock engravings at Cerro de El Riscal or visit the extraordinary Encina de los Perros Natural Monument, an immense tree providing significant shade. Along the Río Tinto, you might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4402997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Río Tinto&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5287308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300427" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Hierro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any long-distance or more challenging hikes in the area?
A: For those seeking a more challenging experience, El Madroño offers routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010392406/el-madrono-miners-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Madroño Miners' Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is nearly 19 km long and considered difficult. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984221964/berrocal-el-madrono-circular-by-the-rio-tinto-and-arroyo-del-gallego" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berrocal - El Madroño – circular by the Río Tinto and Arroyo del Gallego&lt;/a&gt;, spanning over 23 km.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in El Madroño?
A: El Madroño is ideal for hiking due to its tranquil natural surroundings. While specific seasonal data isn't provided, regions in southern Spain generally offer pleasant hiking conditions during spring (March-May) and autumn (September-November) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, and winters are typically mild.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, circular routes are available for hikers. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1144750763/rio-tinto-greenway-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Tinto Greenway Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate circular path. For a longer, more challenging option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984221964/berrocal-el-madrono-circular-by-the-rio-tinto-and-arroyo-del-gallego" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berrocal - El Madroño – circular by the Río Tinto and Arroyo del Gallego&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What makes the flora of El Madroño unique?
A: The region is named after the abundant presence of strawberry trees (madroños), or &lt;em&gt;Arbutus unedo&lt;/em&gt;, which produce edible red berries. Beyond these, the area boasts rich flora, including cork oaks and holm oaks, contributing to a diverse and picturesque natural environment.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in El Madroño?
A: The routes in El Madroño are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from over 70 reviews. Hikers often praise the unique landscapes, the peaceful environment, and the variety of trails available.

Q: Are there options for easy or beginner-friendly hikes?
A: Yes, El Madroño has 5 easy routes suitable for beginners or those looking for a relaxed walk. These paths allow visitors to immerse themselves in the nature and peace of the area without requiring extensive physical exertion.

Q: Is there public transport or parking available for hikers in El Madroño?
A: While specific details on public transport routes are not provided, El Madroño is a small town, and parking is generally available within or near the town center, often serving as a starting point for many trails. It's advisable to check local information upon arrival for the most convenient parking spots.

Q: What is the overall number of hiking trails in the El Madroño area?
A: There are 29 hiking trails available around El Madroño, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: Can I find trails that follow historical paths or old railway lines?
A: Yes, the region's mining history is reflected in some trails. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010556547/rio-tinto-mining-train-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Tinto Mining Train Trail&lt;/a&gt; follows historic railway lines, offering a glimpse into the past while enjoying the unique landscape. Similarly, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1144803253/on-the-banks-of-the-riotinto-along-the-old-railway" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;On the banks of the Riotinto along the old railway&lt;/a&gt; also utilizes old railway paths.
tips: This small but picturesque town in the interior of Seville borders the province of Huelva to the west and you can get here following some paths next to the Mining Basin. Among its places of interest, the Church of San Blas and the beautiful Hermitage next to the Town Hall stand out.
From the bridge over the Gallego stream there is a slight ascent, smooth except for a curved section and with a slope of almost 8%. The landscape has recovered well after the 2004 fire.
The Cachán bridge is an old railway bridge located in Berrocal. Originally it was part of the Rio Tinto railway, which was in operation between 1875 and 1984.

faqs.question: Where does the 'Red waters of the Rio Tinto — circular from El Madrono' trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The trail begins and ends in the village of El Madroño. You should be able to find parking within the village itself, as it's a common starting point for local hikes.

faqs.question: What is the actual difficulty of this trail?
faqs.answer: Komoot rates this trail as 'difficult', primarily due to its length of over 18 km and an elevation gain of around 374 meters. However, local sources often describe similar circular routes in the area as 'moderate'. Expect varied terrain, including descents, ascents through pine forests, and sections along old railway tracks. Appropriate footwear is essential, as some parts may involve walking through scrub or navigating between stones.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, always keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas or near livestock, and ensure you carry enough water for both yourself and your pet, as natural water sources might be limited or unsuitable for drinking due to the Rio Tinto's unique composition.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this trail is during the cooler months, from autumn through spring (October to May). Summers can be very hot in this region, making long hikes challenging. Spring offers pleasant temperatures and potentially greener landscapes contrasting with the red river.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail or the Protected Landscape of the Río Tinto?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this particular circular trail from El Madroño, even though it lies within the Protected Landscape of the Río Tinto. Access to the natural areas is generally free.

faqs.question: What unique features can I expect to see along the way?
faqs.answer: The primary draw is the Rio Tinto itself, with its striking reddish-orange waters and Martian-like landscape, a result of millennia of mining and natural mineral weathering. You'll also traverse old railway tracks, pass by the Cachán Bridge, and get views of historical mining infrastructure. The unique ecosystem of the river, studied by NASA, is also a fascinating aspect.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this hike?
faqs.answer: This circular route is approximately 18.6 kilometers long and typically takes around 5 hours and 15 minutes of moving time to complete. Allow extra time for breaks, photography, and enjoying the unique scenery.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied. You'll experience descents from El Madroño, sections along the Jarrama River, and parts of the Arroyo Gallego leading to the Rio Tinto. Expect to walk on old railway tracks, through pine forests, and potentially over scrub or rocky sections. The path is mostly flat along the 'Sendero de los Mineros' but includes significant elevation changes overall.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly crosses sections of the 'Aldea Las Delgadas a Via Verde del Río Tinto' and passes near the 'Puente Cachá' trail. While the main route is distinct, you'll encounter remnants of the region's extensive network of historical mining paths and railway lines.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restrooms available in El Madroño?
faqs.answer: As El Madroño is the starting and ending point, you should find basic facilities like cafes or small shops there. It's always a good idea to check opening hours, especially in smaller villages, and to carry sufficient water and snacks for the duration of your hike.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's length and varied terrain, bring plenty of water (especially in warmer months), snacks, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing. A map or GPS device is also recommended, as is a fully charged phone. Don't forget your camera to capture the unique red landscapes!

waypoints.name: Touristic Railway for Rio Tinto Mines
